name: De Kas
description: Farm-to-table dining in a magnificent 1926 greenhouse in Frankendael park. The menu changes daily based on what's harvested that morning. One of Amsterdam's most celebrated restaurants.

name: Café de Jaren
description: Grand Amsterdam café on the Amstel river with huge sunny terrace. Legendary lunch spot for locals. Enormous salad bar and some of the city's best club sandwiches.

name: Brouwerij 't IJ
description: Microbrewery inside a functioning windmill in east Amsterdam. Their tripel and witbier are outstanding. The Sunday market draws locals in their hundreds.

name: Rijks Restaurant
description: Fine dining restaurant inside the Rijksmuseum. Michelin-starred chef Dennis Kuipers reinterprets Dutch culinary heritage with precision and creativity.

name: FULU Mandarijn
description: Amsterdam's most refined Chinese restaurant near Nieuwmarkt. Dim sum at lunch and intricate Sichuan and Cantonese dishes at dinner. Excellent value for the quality.

name: Buffet van Odette
description: Beloved neighbourhood bistro in Jordaan. Seasonal Dutch comfort cooking, good natural wine list, and a loyal local following. Reservations essential.

name: Septime
description: Paris's most sought-after natural wine bistro in the 11th. Biodynamic wines, market-driven menu, and a kitchen earning consistent Michelin recognition. Book months ahead.

name: L'As du Fallafel
description: Le Marais institution and arguably the world's best falafel. The pita is loaded with fried aubergine, hummus, and pickled vegetables. Always a queue, always worth it.

name: Café de Flore
description: The most famous café in the world. Existentialist history, people-watching on the terrace, and classic croque monsieurs. Tourist prices, but an essential Paris experience.

name: Au Passage
description: Iconic small plates wine bar in the 11th. Natural wines by the glass, pig's head croquettes, and ambiance that is quintessentially underground Parisian.

name: Bouillon Chartier
description: 1896 brasserie in a spectacular Belle Époque dining room. Classic French brasserie dishes at remarkably low prices. Waiters scribble orders directly onto the paper tablecloth.

name: Frenchie
description: Acclaimed modern French restaurant in the Sentier district. Seasonal tasting menus drawing on international flavours filtered through classical French technique.

name: Bar Pinotxo
description: La Boqueria's legendary breakfast counter. Juanito Bayen has been serving garbanzos, cuttlefish, and house wine since the 1950s. The soul of Barcelona on a stool.

name: El Quim de la Boqueria
description: Market counter serving elevated tapas using whatever's freshest in La Boqueria that morning. Their fried eggs with baby squid is legendary.

name: Bodega Sepúlveda
description: Old-school neighbourhood wine bar in the Eixample. House wine served from the barrel, simple anchovies and jamón, and a genuinely local experience.

name: Tickets
description: Albert Adrià's theatrical tapas bar, sibling to the legendary elBulli. Olive spherification, liquid jamón croquette, and magic in every bite. Notoriously hard to book.

name: La Cova Fumada
description: Birthplace of the bombas (meat-filled potato croquette). Cash only, no menus, no reservations — just authentic Barceloneta seafood prepared as it has been for decades.

name: Cervecería Catalana
description: Barcelona's favourite pintxos bar. Always busy, always excellent. Pan amb tomàquet (bread with tomato), montaditos, and fresh seafood from an open kitchen.

name: Da Enzo al 29
description: The definitive Roman trattoria in Trastevere. Cacio e pepe, carbonara, and coda alla vaccinara prepared exactly as they should be. Book weeks ahead.

name: Supplì Roma
description: Rome's most famous street food, the supplì al telefono (fried rice ball with mozzarella). Eat standing on the pavement on Via di San Francesco a Ripa — the true Roman way.

name: Roscioli
description: An extraordinary Jewish-Roman deli that doubles as an acclaimed restaurant. 3,000-label wine cellar, artisanal cheeses, and a carbonara considered among Rome's best.

name: Grazia & Graziella
description: Straightforward, no-nonsense Roman trattoria near the Testaccio market. Seasonal antipasti, perfectly executed pasta, and tables crammed with local families on weekends.

name: Osteria dell'Enoteca Corsi
description: Hidden gem near the Pantheon serving daily changing Roman classics. Blackboard menu, house wine in carafes, and generous portions at honest prices.

name: Il Sorpasso
description: Prati neighbourhood wine bar and restaurant with a superb natural wine list and creative cicchetti. Buzzing aperitivo scene from 6pm with excellent small plates.

name: Time Out Market
description: Lisbon's famous food hall under the Mercado da Ribeira roof. Over 40 top chefs serving everything from bifanas to bacalhau. The best introduction to Portuguese cuisine.

name: A Cevicheria
description: Chef Kiko Martins fuses Portuguese ingredients with Peruvian technique. The octopus ceviche is transformative. Small room, big waiting list.

name: Solar dos Presuntos
description: Lisbon institution since 1974 in Baixa. Classic dishes like arroz de pato (duck rice), bacalhau à Brás, and presunto from the Trás-os-Montes. Family-run and warmly hospitable.

name: Taberna da Rua das Flores
description: Intimate modern taberna with no written menu — the waiter recites the day's petiscos (small plates) based on the morning market. Outstanding natural Portuguese wines.

name: Pastéis de Belém
description: The original pastel de nata bakery, operating since 1837. The recipe remains secret. Queue for a table or eat standing at the counter with a coffee and cinnamon. Essential.

name: Cervejaria Ramiro
description: Lisbon's most beloved seafood restaurant and a family institution since 1956. Order percebes (barnacles), amêijoas (clams), and the prego (steak sandwich) to finish.
